papers.title: Flying far and fast: the distribution of distant hypervelocity star candidates from Gaia DR2 data
papers.author_names: R. de la Fuente Marcos C. de la Fuente Marcos

abstract.text: Context. Hypervelocity stars move fast enough to leave the gravitational field of their home galaxies and venture into intergalactic space. The most extreme examples known have estimated speeds in excess of 1000 km/s. These can be easily induced at the centres of galaxies via close encounters between binary stars and supermassive black holes; however, a number of other mechanisms operating elsewhere can produce them as well.\\\n  Aims. Recent studies suggest that hypervelocity stars are ubiquitous in the local Universe. In the Milky Way, the known hypervelocity stars are anisotropically distributed, but it is unclear why. Here, we used Gaia Data Release 2 (DR2) data to perform a systematic exploration aimed at confirming or refuting these findings.\\\n  Methods. We assume that the farther the candidate hypervelocity stars are, the more likely they are to be unbound from the Galaxy. We used the statistical analysis of both the spatial distribution and kinematics of these objects to achieve our goals.\\\n  Results. Focussing on nominal Galactocentric distances greater than 30 kpc, which are the most distant candidates, we isolated a sample with speeds in excess of 500 km/s that exhibits a certain degree of anisotropy but remains compatible with possible systematic effects. We find that the effect of the Eddington-Trumpler-Weaver bias is important in our case: over 80% of our sources are probably located further away than implied by their parallaxes; therefore, most of our velocity estimates are lower limits. If this bias is as strong as suggested here, the contamination by disc stars may not affect our overall conclusions.\\\n  Conclusions. The subsample with the lowest uncertainties shows stronger, but obviously systematic, anisotropies and includes a number of candidates of possible extragalactic origin and young age with speeds of up to 2000 km/s.

brief.tldr: [research paper] The study analyzes distant hypervelocity star candidates from Gaia DR2 data at Galactocentric distances >30 kpc, using statistical methods and Monte Carlo sampling to assess their kinematics and spatial distribution. It finds that most velocity estimates are lower limits due to the Eddington-Trumpler-Weaver bias—where parallaxes systematically underestimate distances—leading to underestimation of true velocities; the sample shows anisotropic distribution consistent with systematic effects, and includes candidates with speeds up to 2000 km s⁻¹ that may have extragalactic origins, suggesting a possible population of intergalactic hypervelocity stars.

source_fields.title: Flying far and fast: the distribution of distant hypervelocity star candidates from Gaia DR2 data

source_fields.sections_text: Discussion: The experiment finds that most hypervelocity stars with Galactocentric speeds above 500 km s⁻¹ originate from ejection events within the Milky Way—such as binary interactions, black hole encounters, or tidal debris from disrupted dwarf galaxies—rather than from the galactic halo or extragalactic sources, with key candidates like LAMOST-HVS1 and LAMOST-HVS4 exhibiting velocities of 568±19 km s⁻¹ and 590±7 km s⁻¹, respectively. Conclusions: The spatial distribution of distant hypervelocity star candidates in Gaia DR2 is anisotropic, primarily due to observational biases and selection effects, with most candidates having young ages and likely origins within 100 kpc of the Milky Way, while the Eddington-Trumpler-Weaver bias significantly affects distance and velocity estimates. Introduction: This paper introduces a novel analysis using Gaia DR2 data to resolve the origin of hypervelocity stars by demonstrating that the majority originate outside the Milky Way, addressing the long-standing problem of distinguishing true hypervelocity stars from the high-velocity tail of the galactic halo. Full sample: results: The analysis of Gaia DR2 data reveals that ~65% of stars have Galactocentric speeds above 500 km s⁻¹, indicating a significant population of hypervelocity star candidates, with retrograde motion and high velocities suggesting intergalactic origins, including possible debris from disrupted galaxies or Magellanic Cloud remnants, though large distance uncertainties may bias the observed asymmetries. Best samples: results: The experiment finds that approximately 47% of high-velocity candidates (>$500$ km s⁻¹) in the Gaia DR2 sample are consistent with hypervelocity origins, with a Gaussian distribution of position-velocity angles centered at 90° and average Galactocentric speeds of 786 km s⁻¹, indicating that nearly half of the best-astrometric sources likely originated from the Galactic disc or intergalactic space, with no significant evidence for runaways from the Galactic center. Statistical significance: Despite significant uncertainties in distance and velocity measurements, the statistically significant asymmetry in Galactocentric velocities suggests that many distant hypervelocity stars are likely unbound from the Milky Way. Data selection and processing: This section describes the selection, processing, and statistical analysis of Gaia DR2 data to identify hypervelocity stars beyond 30 kpc from the Galactic center, using median-based measures and Monte Carlo sampling to account for large parallax uncertainties and provide robust kinematic estimates. Appendix A Effects of a systematic parallax zero-point offset: A systematic negative parallax zero-point offset in Gaia DR2 severely undermines the identified hypervelocity star candidates, reducing their fraction from ~30% to ~0.8% after correction, suggesting Gaia DR2 may not be sufficient for detecting such distant stars.

abstract: Hypervelocity stars move fast enough to leave the gravitational field of their home galaxies and venture into intergalactic space.

These can be easily induced at the centres of galaxies via close encounters between binary stars and supermassive black holes; however, a number of other mechanisms operating elsewhere can produce them
Recent studies suggest that hypervelocity stars are ubiquitous in the local Universe. In the Milky Way, the known hypervelocity stars are anisotropically distributed, but it is unclear why.
We assume that the farther the candidate hypervelocity stars are, the more likely they are to be unbound from the Galaxy.
If this bias is as strong as suggested here, the contamination by disc stars may not affect our overall conclusions.\\\n Conclusions.
title: Flying far and fast: the distribution of distant hypervelocity star candidates from Gaia DR2 data

catalog_assessment.evidence: Title and abstract explicitly describe analysis of distant hypervelocity star candidates from Gaia DR2 data, with statistical analysis of spatial distribution and kinematics, indicating a compiled sample of actual stellar objects.

papers.title: Bow shocks, bow waves, and dust waves. IV. Shell shape statistics
papers.author_names: William J. Henney Jorge A. Tarango-Yong Luis Angel Gutierrez-Soto S. J. Arthur

abstract.text: Stellar bow shocks result from relative motions between stars and their environment. The interaction of the stellar wind and radiation with gas and dust in the interstellar medium produces curved arcs of emission at optical, infrared, and radio wavelengths. We recently proposed a new two-dimensional classification scheme for the shape of such bow shocks, which we here apply to three very different observational datasets: mid-infrared arcs around hot OB stars; far-infrared arcs around luminous cool stars; and H alpha emission-line arcs around proplyds and other young stars in the Orion Nebula. For OB stars, the average shape is consistent with simple thin-shell models for the interaction of a spherical wind with a parallel stream, but the diversity of observed shapes is many times larger than such models predict. We propose that this may be caused by time-dependent oscillations in the bow shocks, due to either instabilities or wind variability. Cool star bow shocks have markedly more closed wings than hot star bow shocks, which may be due to the dust emission arising in the shocked stellar wind instead of the shocked interstellar medium. The Orion Nebula arcs, on the other hand, have both significantly more open wings and significantly flatter apexes than the hot star bow shocks. We test several possible explanations for this difference (divergent ambient stream, low Mach number, observational biases, and influence of collimated jets), but the evidence for each is inconclusive.

triage.label: rule-weak-llm-confirmed
triage.reason: Weak title match: runaway-related mechanisms LLM confirmed: Focuses on bow shocks from OB stars with relative motion, relevant for runaway/high-velocity stars

brief.skipped_reason: weak rule match; retained with search metadata only

source_fields.sections_text: Summary: This study finds that stellar bow shocks exhibit diverse shapes depending on stellar type, with significant deviations from theoretical models attributed to oscillations, ambient flow conditions, and observational biases. Footnotes: The footnotes provide author contact information, references to online resources, and explanatory notes on stellar classifications, data distributions, and statistical considerations in the analysis. Appendix#2: This appendix presents a simplified model of small, time-varying perturbations to a steady-state bow shock shape, characterized by standing waves with specific wave numbers and amplitudes, and examines how these oscillations affect the shock's projected appearance at different inclinations. Discussion: The median observed bow shock planitude and alatitude (Π = 1.57, Λ = 1.69) are consistent with the thin-shell wilkinoid model predictions, but the large observed dispersion (σ(Π) = 0.87, σ(Λ) = 0.30)—exceeding model predictions by factors of 5 and 3—indicates that the model fails to account for the diversity in shock shapes, particularly in apex and wing morphology. Introduction: This paper introduces a novel diagnostic method using bow shock size and luminosity ratio to distinguish between bow shocks driven by stellar wind, radiation pressure, or cavity illumination, thereby solving the problem of ambiguous identification of bow shocks in observational data. Stationary Emission Line Arcs In M42: Stationary emission line arcs in the Orion Nebula exhibit significantly higher planitude and alatitude compared to OB star bow shocks, with a bimodal shape distribution indicating a distinct population of arcs resembling OB stars and others with more pronounced bow shock geometries. Far-Infrared Arcs Around Late-Type Stars: This section identifies and analyzes far-infrared arc structures ("fermata-like" or Class I) in circumstellar shells around late-type AGB and supergiant stars, observed via the Herschel MESS survey, revealing clear bow shock features in symmetric, arc-shaped emission. Mid-Infrared Arcs Around Early-Type Stars: This section describes a method for automatically tracing and fitting mid-infrared bow shock arcs around early-type stars, using MIPS 24μm data from the MIPSGAL survey to analyze their shapes, positions, and curvatures, with results showing good agreement with previous studies.

For OB stars, the average shape is consistent with simple thin-shell models for the interaction of a spherical wind with a parallel stream, but the diversity of observed shapes is many times larger than

catalog_assessment.evidence: Paper focuses on classification and statistical analysis of bow shock shapes across different stellar populations, not on presenting object-level observational data for high-velocity stars themselves.

papers.title: Mergers of globular clusters in the Galactic disc: intermediate mass black hole coalescence and implications for gravitational waves detection

abstract.text: We propose a new formation channel for intermediate mass black hole (IMBH) binaries via globular cluster collisions in the Galactic disc. Using numerical simulations, we show that the IMBHs form a tight binary that enters the gravitational waves (GWs) emission dominated regime driven by stellar interactions, and ultimately merge in $\\\\lesssim 0.5$ Gyr. These events are clearly audible to LISA and can be associated with electromagnetic emission during the last evolutionary stages. During their orbital evolution, the IMBHs produce runaway stars comparable with GAIA and LAMOST observations.

triage.label: rule-weak-llm-confirmed
triage.reason: Weak title match: stellar interactions in dense systems LLM confirmed: Explicitly mentions production of runaway stars from IMBH binaries in globular cluster collisions

brief.skipped_reason: weak rule match; retained with search metadata only

source_fields.sections_text: II Method: The paper proposes a high-resolution N-body simulation method that models the long-term evolution of 100 globular clusters in a realistic Galactic potential, incorporating the dynamics of intermediate-mass black holes and using parallel computing to accurately track both cluster orbits and black hole binary evolution. III Results: A massive IMBH binary forms within ∼1 Gyr after Galactic globular cluster collisions and merges on a timescale of ∼677 Myr, with a merger rate of ∼2–4 IMBH binaries per MW-like galaxy over a Hubble time, making such events potentially detectable via gravitational waves. I Introduction: This paper introduces a novel mechanism for IMBH binary formation through the merger of two globular clusters orbiting the Galactic disc, demonstrating that such mergers efficiently produce detectable low-frequency gravitational waves via LISA and can explain observed runaway stars. IV Conclusions: This study proposes and simulates a mechanism where collisions between globular clusters in the Galactic disc form intermediate-mass black hole binaries, which efficiently harden via stellar scatterings, emit detectable gravitational waves observable by LISA, and merge on a timescale of ~1 Gyr, predicting 2–4 such mergers per MW-like galaxy per Hubble time.

abstract: During their orbital evolution, the IMBHs produce runaway stars comparable with GAIA and LAMOST observations.

catalog_assessment.evidence: Purely theoretical/simulation paper proposing formation channel for IMBH binaries via globular cluster collisions, with only passing mention that simulations produce runaway stars comparable to observations.

papers.title: Mass-loss rates for O and early B stars powering bowshock nebulae: evidence for bi-stability behavior
papers.author_names: Henry A. Kobulnicky William T. Chick Matthew S. Povich

abstract.text: Second only to initial mass, the rate of wind-driven mass loss determines the final mass of a massive star and the nature of its remnant. Motivated by the need to reconcile observational values and theory, we use a recently vetted technique to analyze the mass-loss rates in a sample of OB stars that generate bowshock nebulae. We measure peculiar velocities from new Gaia parallax and proper motion data and their spectral types from new optical and infrared spectroscopy. For our sample of 67 central stars in morphologically selected bowshocks nebulae, 67 are OB stars. The median peculiar velocity is 11 km/s, significantly smaller than classical `runaway star' velocities. Mass-loss rates for these O and early B stars agree with recently lowered theoretical predictions, ranging from ~10^-7 Msun/yr for mid-O dwarfs to 10^-9 Msun/yr for late-O dwarfs---a factor of about 2.7 lower than the often-used Vink et al. (2001) formulation. Our results provide the first observational mass-loss rates for B0--B3 dwarfs and giants---10^-9 to 10^-8 Msun/yr. We find evidence for an increase in the mass-loss rates below a critical effective temperature, consistent with predictions of the bi-stability phenomenon in the range Teff=19,000--27,000 K. The sample exhibits a correlation between modified wind momentum and luminosity, consistent in slope but lower by 0.43 dex in magnitude compared to canonical wind-luminosity relations. We identify a small subset of objects deviating most significantly from theoretical expectations as probable radiation-driven bow wave nebulae by virtue of their low stellar-to-nebular luminosity ratios. For these, the inferred mass-loss rates must be regarded as upper limits.

triage.label: rule-weak-llm-confirmed
triage.reason: Weak title match: runaway-related mechanisms LLM confirmed: Explicitly studies OB stars with bowshock nebulae, measures peculiar velocities, and discusses runaway stars. Median peculiar velocity is 11 km/s, which is lower than classical runaway velocities but still relevant to high-velocity populations.

brief.skipped_reason: weak rule match; retained with search metadata only

source_fields.sections_text: Analysis: This section analyzes mass-loss rates of stars as a function of effective temperature, showing that observed rates generally align with theoretical models—particularly Krticka's predictions—while highlighting systematic deviations and the influence of stellar evolution and wind clumping. New Data: New optical and infrared spectroscopic observations of candidate bowshock stars were obtained at the Apache Point Observatory with high signal-to-noise ratios and precise radial velocity measurements, enabling detailed spectral analysis and velocity calibration. Footnotes: This section discusses adjustments to effective temperatures and notes exceptions in stellar data, focusing on the first bistability jump and the use of improved observational data for specific stars. Discussion: The experiment finds that mass-loss rates for 67 early-type stars, derived from momentum balance with the ISM, agree well with theoretical models (especially those including bi-stability) above 25,000 K, with mean rates ~2.7 times lower than the Vink et al. (2001) prescription and significant deviations in cooler stars likely due to photoevaporation, radiation-driven bow waves, or observational biases, resulting in a large dispersion (average uncertainty of 42%) and suggesting that mass-loss rates are best interpreted as upper or lower limits depending on environmental conditions. Introduction: This paper introduces a novel approach to diagnosing mass loss in massive stars by leveraging X-ray and infrared spectroscopy to bridge the gap between observed and theoretical mass-loss rates, particularly addressing the weak-wind problem and the long-standing lack of observational verification of the bi-stability jump in stellar winds. SAMPLE SELECTION FOR MEASURING $\\dot{M}$: After selecting 94 bowshock candidate stars with reliable distances, proper motions, and 70 μm nebulae detections, and obtaining spectral types for 70 of them, the sample is used to estimate mass loss rates ($\\dot{M}$), with distance uncertainties and stellar temperatures carefully accounted for to minimize biases and improve accuracy.

title: Mass-loss rates for O and early B stars powering bowshock nebulae: evidence for bi-stability behavior
